response:
## Decision: Run the full pre-registered grid (Option A) — no early stop on a single-arm pilot

**Choice.** Execute all 4 arms (Haiku-solo / Sonnet-solo / Opus-solo / Tiered) across the pre-screened 8 tasks × 3 reps as pre-registered (ISSUE-41.md:33-43, 68-69). The only sanctioned adaptivity is the pre-registered sequential-escalation clause (line 49) — add reps/tasks when a CI straddles the boundary — applied within this fixed 4-arm design. No arm is piloted alone, and no arm is skipped or deferred based on an early read.

**Rejected: pilot Opus-solo (or any single arm) first and stop early if the gate looks decidable.** The decision rule at line 51 is a function of Tiered and Sonnet-solo only (delta vs Sonnet-solo, tokens/wall-clock vs Sonnet-solo). No single-arm pilot — including the "most expensive" one — can ever produce a decidable read on that rule; at minimum Tiered+Sonnet-solo must run together. A pilot framed as "stop early if the gate is decidable" is therefore not a leaner version of the same test, it's a different, non-load-bearing measurement (Opus-solo alone speaks only to the secondary "does Tiered reach Opus-solo quality" report) dressed up as if it could settle adoption.

**The single load-bearing assumption:** *the sequential-escalation clause at line 49 ("add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous") is scoped to reps/tasks within the fixed 4-arm design, not to skipping or reordering whole arms.* Everything about running the full grid rather than piloting rests on reading line 49 narrowly. If that's wrong — if the owner actually intended line 49's escalation license to license arm-level triage (e.g., "don't bother running Haiku-solo/Opus-solo until the Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo comparison is inconclusive") — then this decision over-spends: it runs ~250-300 invocations up front (line 71) when a leaner staged design (Tiered+Sonnet-solo first, escalate to Haiku-solo/Opus-solo only if needed) would have sufficed and cost meaningfully less.

**The failure class I'm accepting:** over-spend / lower efficiency, not invalidity. Worst case, this run spends tokens on Haiku-solo and Opus-solo data that turns out to be unnecessary because Tiered vs Sonnet-solo alone was already unambiguous — pure cost against the ~250-300-invocation estimate. I'm accepting this over the alternative failure class (Assumption Probe's Option B risk, sharpened by the Premortem Probe): an arm-order stopping rule is an undisclosed second adaptive-stopping mechanism layered on top of the one pre-registered, reopening the exact fishing risk pre-registration exists to close (line 25/27), and it risks the pre-screen's Haiku-Opus gradient check (line 42) and the quality-vs-cost frontier (line 48) being silently left incomplete while ADR 0024's "record the result either way" (line 18) reads as if the full pre-registered test ran. Between paying for data that might go unused and shipping a study whose completeness and provenance can't be defended under later audit, the former is the correct trade — pre-registration's value comes from running the committed design, not from being maximally cheap.

**Not resolved by this decision, and blocking regardless of arm order:** the owner call at line 73 item 1 (grader independence is unsolvable with 3 tiers once Opus-solo runs — accept the Sonnet-grades-Sonnet residual or defer). This call is upstream of *any* arm running, including a pilot, and must be made before Opus-solo data is generated under either option.

premortem.response:
Three plausible failure classes if "pilot Opus-solo first, stop early" ships and the study is later relied on:

**1. The pilot answers a different question than the one that gates adoption.** The pre-registered decision rule (ISSUE-41.md:51) requires *delta vs Sonnet-solo* AND a *tokens-vs-Sonnet-solo* ratio — both depend on the Sonnet-solo and Tiered arms, not Opus-solo. Opus-solo alone can only speak to the secondary question flagged in that same line ("whether Tiered reaches Opus-solo quality at sub-Opus cost"). Six months out, the plausible failure is that whoever ran the pilot treated "Opus looks clearly better/worse" as if it settled the primary adoption call, stopped there, and the actual pre-registered hypothesis (line 31) was never tested — while the ADR 0024 append-only record (line 18, "Record the result either way") reads as if it was.

**2. It silently breaks the pre-screen's dependency on the Haiku endpoint.** Task selection (line 42) is explicitly a *1-rep Haiku-solo vs Opus-solo* comparison to drop saturated tasks, and the cost/time analysis (line 48) requires placing "all 4 arms on a quality-vs-cost frontier." If "pilot the most expensive arm first" gets read as reordering or replacing that pre-screen step, tokens get spent on 3-rep Opus-solo runs before anyone confirms a Haiku-Opus gradient exists on those tasks, and early stop-outs leave the frontier permanently missing its cheap floor — undermining the exact comparison the frontier plot exists to make.

**3. It stacks a second, undocumented adaptive-stopping rule on top of the one already pre-registered, reopening the fishing risk pre-registration exists to close.** Comment 1 opens by saying the plan was "registered before running to avoid fishing for a favorable cut" (line 27), and it already specifies one sequential-escalation mechanism: "add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary" (line 49). An *arm-order* stopping rule ("pilot most expensive, stop if the gate is decidable") isn't that rule — it's a second, ad hoc one layered on top, decided at execution time rather than pre-registered. This also collides with the still-open owner call on grader independence (line 73, item 1: "unsolvable with 3 tiers" since Opus-solo has no distinct-model grader available per line 44's requirement) — piloting Opus-solo before that call is resolved means the pilot's grading convention is itself undefined, so an early stop is being decided on results whose grading methodology can't be defended under later audit.

assumption.response:
Two realistic options for the execution-design call, each with its single load-bearing dependency:

**Option A — Run the full pre-registered grid** (4 arms × 8 tasks × 3 reps, ISSUE-41.md:33-43,68-69), only escalating reps/tasks per the sequential-escalation clause (line 49).
- Load-bearing dependency: that Haiku-solo and Opus-solo data are required *in this run* to satisfy the pre-registration, not just the adoption decision. The decision rule itself (line 51) only consumes Tiered vs Sonnet-solo; Haiku-solo/Opus-solo feed the frontier (line 48) and the "Opus quality at sub-Opus cost" report (line 51), which are framed as reportable, not gating.
- **If X is false, Option A flips**: if the floor/ceiling arms (Haiku-solo, Opus-solo) are *not* load-bearing for the adoption call itself and can be added later without re-running solos, then running all 4 arms up front is over-spend against the ~250-300-invocation cost estimate (line 71) — a leaner design would suffice.

**Option B — Pilot the decision-critical arms first (Tiered + Sonnet-solo, the pair the decision rule actually uses), stop early if the gate at line 51 is already decidable, defer Haiku-solo/Opus-solo.**
- Load-bearing dependency: that the sequential-escalation clause (line 49 — "add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous") extends in scope to *skipping/deferring whole arms*, not just adding reps/tasks within the fixed 4-arm design as written.
- **If X is false, Option B flips**: if line 49's escalation license is narrowly scoped to reps/tasks within the pre-registered arm set, then dropping arms mid-run is an undisclosed deviation from the pre-registration — the exact fishing risk the pre-registration exists to guard against (line 25) — not a sanctioned lean variant.

Note: "the most expensive arm" is itself underspecified in the materials — Tiered (multi-agent, ≤3 cycles) and Opus-solo (per-token cost) are both candidates, and the decision rule needs Tiered *paired with* Sonnet-solo regardless, so a single-arm-only pilot can't produce a decidable delta under line 51's rule.
